metadata.title: The Base Rate Problem: Why 0.68 Is Not 68%
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut of Reallocation Engine candidate 07. Source chapter: books/the-reallocation-engine/chapters/05-verifying-the-data.md (base-rate / posterior-correction mechanism only).
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL #1F6F5C = corrected posterior / true probability / the honest number; CRIMSON #BF3339 = raw score / overstatement / the gap; GOLD = editor highlight once per graphic; never swap mid-film

style_bible.visual_style: editorial paper collage on real newsprint scan; desaturated plates; flat annotation plane; serif labels with hairline underlines; hand-drawn annotation ring used once per graphic
style_bible.color_palette: cream ground, charcoal serif type, TEAL = corrected posterior / true probability, CRIMSON = raw score / overstatement, gold highlighter once

metadata.note: Card exclusions honored: NO Bayes theorem formula on screen; NO odds-form calculation; NO calibration curve detail; NO four base-rate diagnostic questions as a list — just the base-rate one illustrated. Mechanism carried by visual counts, not equations. Numbers from chapter: SIC prior 0.08, raw score 0.68, posterior approx 0.38-0.45. The 1000-company example is illustrative (labeled as such). Cambridge biotech: 12 LCA filings, 80% H-1B approval rate, pharmaceutical/medicine-manufacturing SIC code, 8% SIC-level LCA filing rate.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

skin_warnings: B01: palette=claude but the cold open is 'un-annotated' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B15: palette=claude but the outro is 'OutroCTA' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. The sponsorship score comes back 0.68. Sixty-eight percent. Strong signal — right? But the actual probability this company will sponsor you is closer to 40.
beats.new_visual_element: [TITLE] The Base Rate Problem / Why 0.68 Is Not 68%

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [TITLE] The Base Rate Problem / Why 0.68 Is Not 68%

beats.narration_text: A mid-size Cambridge biotech. Twelve LCA filings over three years. Eighty percent H-1B approval rate. The scorer returns 0.68. Every record real, every signal verified.

props.headline: A mid-size Cambridge biotech. Twelve LCA filings over three years. Eighty…

beats.scene_description: a Cambridge, Massachusetts biotech lab exterior or research building — brick facade, professional, editorial newsprint treatment, slow push-in
beats.image_prompt: B02, mid-size Cambridge Massachusetts biotech company building exterior, brick and glass research facility, overcast natural light, professional, desaturated editorial newsprint treatment, flat editorial collage, no people

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [STILL] Cambridge biotech building exterior

beats.narration_text: Twelve filings. Eighty percent approval. Score: 0.68. The numbers check out. So why is that 0.68 telling you something that isn't true?
beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] filing stats card: 12 filings, 80% approval, score 0.68 in crimson

beats.narration_text: A calibrated sponsorship score should reflect actual probability. Here is the case where 0.68 means closer to 0.40. Why?

card.copy: A score of 0.68 should mean a 68% chance of sponsorship.
card.sub: Here is the case where it means closer to 40%. Why?

beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] THE QUESTION beat — gap formula on screen

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] THE QUESTION beat — gap formula on screen

beats.narration_text: The SIC code matters. In pharmaceutical and medicine manufacturing, only 8 percent of employers have ever filed an LCA. Nine out of ten never sponsor at all.
beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] isotype grid: 100 squares, 8 teal (filed LCA), 92 slate (never filed)

beats.narration_text: Start at 8 percent. That is your prior before you see any signal at all. The scorer hasn't run yet. You already know most companies here are not sponsors.
beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] horizontal probability axis 0 to 1, single teal marker at 0.08, labeled PRIOR

beats.narration_text: Now the signal arrives: twelve filings, strong approval rate. The scorer says 0.68. It is working in the right direction. But the signal is pushing against a prior that says: start at 8, not at 50.
beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] three markers appear on probability axis: prior 0.08 teal-left, raw score 0.68 crimson-right, gap arrow sweeping left

beats.narration_text: The prior pulls the estimate back. Not all the way to 8 percent — the signal is real and it counts. But the correction lands around 40 percent. The raw score overstates by 50 to 75 percent.
beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] correction arrow sweeps from 0.68 leftward to ~0.40 teal marker, posterior labeled CORRECTED

beats.narration_text: A signal is only as strong as the base rate it works against. In a field where 92 percent of employers never sponsor, even a strong filing record produces a lower probability than the raw score implies.

card.copy: A signal is only as strong as the base rate it works against.
card.sub: 92% of employers in this SIC code never sponsor.

beats.narration_text: The 0.68 didn't know it was working against a 0.08 prior. It was trained on filing signals, not on the base rate of the SIC code. That gap is where the overstatement lives.
beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] probability axis with all three markers; GOLD highlight sweep on the gap between 0.68 and 0.40; label 'the overstatement'

beats.narration_text: Imagine 1,000 companies in this SIC code. Only 80 have ever filed an LCA. The scorer flags 100 as likely sponsors. But of those 100 flags, how many are real?
beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] isotype grid: 1000 squares, 80 teal (true filers), 920 slate (never filed) — illustrative

beats.narration_text: Even at 80 percent precision, only 64 of those 100 flags are genuine sponsors. Thirty-six are false positives — companies the scorer called likely that never filed. The raw 0.68 didn't account for those 36.
beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] 100 flagged squares: 64 teal (genuine), 36 crimson (false positives) — illustrative

beats.narration_text: A calibrated score should reflect actual probability. In a SIC code where only 8 percent ever sponsor, 0.68 means closer to 0.40. The base rate pulls it back.

card.sub: The base rate pulls the signal back toward the prior. — THE REALLOCATION ENGINE

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] endcard recap: question answered
